stories.body: Israel and Iran appeared to step back from renewed direct attacks after Donald Trump urged both sides to “immediately stop shooting,” but the ceasefire remains fragile as fighting continues around Lebanon and maritime tensions rise near the Strait of Hormuz. Reuters reported that U.S. forces struck Iranian coastal radar sites at Goruk and Qeshm Island after shooting down Iranian drones that U.S. officials said were aimed at maritime traffic, while Iran has demanded that Israel withdraw from southern Lebanon and has made a Hezbollah-Israel ceasefire part of any broader deal with Washington. Hezbollah said it attacked Israeli troops in south Lebanon, Israeli strikes hit towns across the south, and Israeli officials said they would keep operating against Hezbollah despite U.S. pressure. The broader conflict has left shipping through Hormuz sharply reduced, driven up energy costs, and exposed gaps in U.S.-arranged ceasefires that Trump described as “shooting in a more moderate manner” rather than a full halt.
stories.headline: Israel-Iran ceasefire tested as fighting spreads to Lebanon and Hormuz

stories.body: Rescuers in the southern Philippines are searching collapsed buildings and landslide-hit communities after a magnitude 7.8 earthquake struck off Sarangani province, killing at least 37 people and injuring hundreds. The quake hit Mindanao on Monday morning, damaging homes, schools, hospitals, roads and power lines, with General Santos City among the worst affected and some areas of Sarangani reachable only by helicopter. Authorities said tens of thousands of people were affected or displaced, tsunami warnings were issued and later lifted across parts of the Philippines, Indonesia, Malaysia and Japan, and residents were warned not to re-enter damaged buildings because hundreds of aftershocks have followed, including a magnitude 6.5 tremor. Officials said the death toll could rise as rescue teams reach more communities and assess thousands of damaged public school buildings before classes can resume.
stories.headline: Powerful Philippines earthquake kills dozens as aftershocks continue

stories.body: Chinese President Xi Jinping completed a two-day visit to Pyongyang, his first official trip to North Korea since 2019, with both sides pledging deeper strategic cooperation but announcing no major concrete deals. Kim Jong Un gave Xi a lavish welcome with a red carpet, military honors, performances and a banquet, while state media said the leaders discussed trade, agriculture, construction, technology and closer high-level exchanges. The visit coincides with the 65th anniversary of the countries’ mutual defense treaty, China’s only formal defense pact, and comes as Beijing seeks to reassert influence over North Korea after Pyongyang moved closer to Russia through weapons transfers, troop deployments and a mutual defense pact with Moscow. State media accounts notably avoided public emphasis on denuclearization, reflecting China’s softer recent posture on that issue as it tries to keep North Korea within its orbit.
stories.headline: Xi and Kim pledge closer China-North Korea ties during rare Pyongyang summit

stories.body: OpenAI said it has confidentially filed with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission for a possible initial public offering, joining Anthropic and SpaceX in a rush of major technology listings driven by enormous capital needs. The ChatGPT maker said it has not decided on timing and may remain private for a while because some work is easier outside public markets, but said the filing gives it the option to move sooner if that becomes best. The announcement comes as OpenAI and Anthropic compete for users, enterprise customers and investors, with recent private valuations reported at $852 billion for OpenAI and $965 billion for Anthropic. Analysts said the companies’ demand for cash to fund chips, infrastructure and model training is pushing them toward public markets, where investors will scrutinize their financials and product pipelines more closely.
stories.headline: OpenAI files confidentially for IPO as AI fundraising race accelerates

stories.body: The U.S. Department of Defense added Alibaba, BYD, Baidu and other major Chinese companies to its Section 1260H list of firms it says have ties to China’s military, a move that does not immediately impose sanctions but warns U.S. organizations of national security risks and can affect defense contracting. The updated list now names 188 “Chinese military companies,” including firms accused of contributing to Beijing’s military-civil fusion strategy through state-linked programs and affiliations. China’s embassy in Washington called the designations discriminatory and said Chinese firms comply with host-country laws, while Alibaba and Baidu rejected the claims and said there was no credible basis for their inclusion. The move risks worsening U.S.-China tensions shortly after a Trump-Xi summit aimed at easing trade and technology frictions.
stories.headline: US blacklists major Chinese firms over alleged military ties, drawing Beijing’s condemnation

stories.body: The U.S. Senate passed a bill providing $70 billion in additional immigration enforcement funding for the Department of Homeland Security, sending it to the House without language to permanently block Donald Trump’s controversial $1.8 billion “anti-weaponization” fund. The measure passed 52-47 with no Democratic support and one Republican opposing it, after failed amendment efforts by Democrats and some Republicans to eliminate the fund or redirect its money. Senate Republican leader John Thune said the issue was settled because acting Attorney General Todd Blanche had testified that the Justice Department would not move forward with the fund, but Democrats argued that a promise from Trump’s ally was insufficient, especially after Trump said the fund was important. The bill would finance ICE and Border Patrol operations for Trump’s deportation crackdown, even as protests and legal fights over detention conditions and immigration restrictions continue in New Jersey and federal court.
stories.headline: Senate passes $70 billion immigration enforcement bill without blocking Trump fund

stories.body: Maine Democrats are split over Senate candidate Graham Platner days before a primary he is still expected to win, after new reports about extramarital sexting, allegations from former partners, past Reddit posts and a tattoo resembling a Nazi symbol deepened concerns about his viability against Republican Sen. Susan Collins. Politico reported that some voters who had backed Platner now feel let down, while others see the controversies as attacks on a working-class outsider whose campaign focuses on universal health care, money in politics and affordability. Democratic strategists and donors are debating whether a weak primary showing could pressure Platner to step aside so the state party can replace him before July, but his allies, including Bernie Sanders, Elizabeth Warren, Ro Khanna and many grassroots supporters, remain publicly supportive. The stakes are high because Maine is the only blue-state Republican Senate seat Democrats are targeting this cycle, and losing it could leave the party with a much harder path to a Senate majority.
stories.headline: Maine Democrats divided over Graham Platner as primary nears

stories.body: Louisiana Republicans passed a new congressional map that would eliminate one of the state’s two Democratic majority-Black districts before the midterms, sending the plan to Gov. Jeff Landry, who is expected to sign it. The move follows a Supreme Court decision narrowing the Voting Rights Act and is part of a broader mid-decade redistricting fight that began in Texas and has spread to multiple states. Rep. Cleo Fields’ district would be erased, while Rep. Troy Carter’s New Orleans-based district would remain more similar to its earlier shape, and Louisiana has postponed House primaries to November under a state emergency declaration. In New York, Democrats are responding with a proposed constitutional amendment that would allow mid-decade congressional redistricting and remove a ban on lines drawn to favor parties, while also seeking to give the Legislature authority over ballot language for constitutional amendments, a move Republicans say would politicize the referendum process.
stories.headline: Republican redistricting advances in Louisiana as New York Democrats seek countermeasures

stories.body: Pope Leo XIV’s visit to Spain has taken on political weight as his speeches aligned with major themes of Pedro Sánchez’s government, including a pro-immigration message, opposition to war and calls to lower Spain’s harsh political rhetoric. In a historic address to Congress and the Senate, the pope urged public officials to “disarm language,” warned against the permanent disqualification of opponents and criticized discrimination against immigrants, drawing a long ovation in a chamber known for polarization. At the Spanish bishops’ conference, he also described clerical child abuse as a “plague” and called for sincere listening, protection, justice, reparation and “real changes of healing,” marking a clearer public stance than many victims’ groups had heard from Francis during Spain’s abuse reckoning. His Madrid program ended with a large meeting of volunteers at IFEMA before he departed for Barcelona.
stories.headline: Pope Leo XIV’s Spain visit becomes a political moment on immigration, war and abuse

stories.body: Several investigations touching Spain’s Socialist orbit widened this week, with El País reporting that notebooks seized from former PSOE member Leire Díez contain first-hand information about public companies including SEPI, Correos, Tragsa and Efe, and references to PSOE president Cristina Narbona, who has been called as a witness. A Madrid judge also admitted PP and Vox complaints over the public rescue of Air Europa and summoned SEPI vice president Bartolomé Lora as a suspect in an administrative prevarication case. Separately, the Kitchen trial resumed with former police commissioner José Manuel Villarejo questioned after investigators said he met Díez several times in 2024 and was allegedly offered a deal involving information about judges and political figures. El Periódico reported that Judge José Luis Calama has asked U.S. authorities for permission to use phone evidence supplied by Homeland Security Investigations in a case involving José Luis Rodríguez Zapatero, Plus Ultra and alleged influence-peddling and money laundering linked to the Air Europa rescue.
stories.headline: Spain’s PSOE-linked corruption investigations widen around Leire Díez, SEPI and Air Europa

stories.body: Pope Leo XIV arrives in Barcelona from Madrid for a two-day visit centered on the Sagrada Família, where he is expected to bless and inaugurate the tower of Jesus, while also visiting the Cathedral, the Olympic Stadium, Sant Agustí in the Raval, Brians prison and Montserrat. The visit has triggered a major security and mobility operation, with traffic cuts and parking restrictions around Ciutat Vella, Montjuïc, the Raval and the Sagrada Família, plus metro reinforcements, shuttle buses and an extended transit schedule. The disruption coincides with university entrance exams and a teachers’ strike, prompting the Generalitat to recommend telework for those who normally commute into Barcelona. The visit has also revived debate over the role of Catalan in papal liturgies after a leaked Sagrada Família script appeared to favor Spanish, though Catalan officials say Leo XIV is sensitive to Catalonia’s reality and that this will be reflected publicly.
stories.headline: Barcelona prepares for Pope Leo XIV amid transport disruption, protests and security concerns

stories.body: Barcelona officials are calling for more police and tougher penalties after a second fatal shooting in less than a month on Carrer de la Mineria in the Zona Franca area. Mossos d’Esquadra are investigating the killing of a man shot on Sunday night in La Marina de Port, with local reports citing investigators who suspect links to organized crime and Eastern European mafia disputes, though police have not connected the case to a previous May shooting on the same street. Mayor Jaume Collboni said drug trafficking and organized crime exceed the capacity of any city hall and argued for more officers and higher prison sentences for weapons and drug offenses, while Interior minister Núria Parlon said the government would meet the city after the pope’s visit and consider reinforcing the Kanpai policing plan in the area. City officials have tried to reassure residents that the violence is tied to criminal vendettas rather than neighborhood dynamics, while promising more Mossos and Guàrdia Urbana presence.
stories.headline: Barcelona seeks stronger policing after second fatal Zona Franca shooting in a month
